---
title: "Sensitivity to Subjective Expected Utility Maximization: A Methodological Study, with an Illustrative Application to LLM Decision-Making"
author:
  - name: Jeff Helzner
abstract: |
  Evaluating the quality of decisions made under uncertainty is hard when
  labeled outcomes are scarce, costly, or confounded with luck. We treat
  subjective expected utility (SEU) maximization as a *stated standard* and
  define a graded measure --- *SEU sensitivity* --- of an agent's conformity to
  it. The formal vehicle is a softmax choice model with a sensitivity parameter
  $\alpha$ applied to SEU-valued alternatives; the methodological contribution
  is a sequence of identifiability results for $\alpha$ and for the belief and
  utility parameters $(\beta, \delta)$, validated in Stan via prior predictive
  checks, parameter recovery, and simulation-based calibration (SBC). The
  findings are reported with their finite-sample caveats intact. In the
  uncertain-choice-only model
  `m_0`, $\alpha$ is identifiable but $(\beta, \delta)$ are only weakly
  informed by uncertain choices: the joint posterior barely contracts and
  concentrates on a $\beta$--$\delta$ trade-off at realistic sample sizes. In
  the extended model `m_1`, $\delta$ becomes
  identifiable in principle (hence estimable in the limit) via a $\beta$-free
  risky block, but its practical recovery gain at realistic sample sizes is
  negligible (a matched-count CI-width reduction under 1%); and at matched choice
  count the $\beta$-free risky block yields no $\alpha$-precision gain either, so
  finite-sample $\alpha$ precision is driven by data
  *quantity*, not the *type* of choice. These are two distinct phenomena: for
  $\delta$, identifiability does not imply precise estimability at realistic
  $n$; and for $\alpha$, identifiability in principle does not by itself reveal
  what governs its precision at finite $n$ --- which we find to be the
  *quantity* of choice data, not the *type* of choice. Marginal SBC passes for both models even
  where the joint (belief, utility) posterior is only weakly informed --- a
  *marginal-SBC demarcation* we make precise. A two-by-two illustrative
  application (GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, each on insurance-claims triage and
  Ellsberg-style urns, with sampling temperature as the lever) shows the
  workflow runs end-to-end on real LLM choice data, detecting a structured
  comparative $\alpha$ effect in two of four cells and declining to support one
  in the other two.
keywords: [subjective expected utility, identifiability, simulation-based calibration, Bayesian workflow, softmax choice, LLM decision-making]
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: letter
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
    toc: true
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}
        \usepackage{bm}
        \usepackage[htt]{hyphenat}
        \newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
        \newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
        \newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
        \newtheorem{corollary}{Corollary}
        \theoremstyle{definition}
        \newtheorem{definition}{Definition}
        \theoremstyle{remark}
        \newtheorem{remark}{Remark}
---

<!--
Drafting status: formal core + implementation half + application + discussion +
appendices (A–E). All sections §§1–8 and Appendices A–E drafted. Sections are
numbered manually to match the plan outline; auto-numbering is off.
-->

{{< include sections/_01_motivation.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_02_abstract_model.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_03_m0_identifiability.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_04_m0_implementation.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_05_m1_identifiability.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_06_m1_implementation.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_07_application.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_08_discussion.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_appendix_a.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_appendix_b.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_appendix_c.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_appendix_d.qmd >}}

{{< include sections/_appendix_e.qmd >}}
